summary: Draft next-wave map built from v0.3, now testing Battery Park, Grove St, Hudson Yards, Flushing, Newark Airport, Red Hook, and Union Square while tightening central overlap and removing a few realism-breaking direct shortcuts.
designGoals: Preserve v0.3 as the stable 13-station baseline while the next wave explores new Manhattan, Queens, and Jersey nodes.
Keep the board graph-first and readable while testing west-Manhattan and outer-ring additions without collapsing into central overlap.
Prefer board-game compression over literal geography, but remove direct links that break real-world transit intuition too sharply.
Use controlled station waves so each addition justifies its strategy and space on the board.
changeSummary: Branched a new draft from the frozen v0.3-atlantic-hoboken release.
Added Battery Park as the first south-core next-wave station.
Split the old World Trade to Downtown Brooklyn corridor into World Trade -> Battery Park -> Downtown Brooklyn.
Added Grove St as the first Jersey-side next-wave station and split Newark Penn -> Exchange Place into Newark Penn -> Grove St -> Exchange Place.
Recast the former Columbus Circle slot as Hudson Yards, a west-Manhattan ring node linked to Grand Central.
Added Hoboken -> Grove St to make the west-side graph read as a more coherent Jersey corridor.
Added Flushing as the first outer-Queens ring node, linked to Grand Central and Jamaica.
Added Newark Airport as a southwest outer node linked to Newark Penn, then extended it with a difficult Hudson Yards helicopter-style tunnel route.
Added Red Hook as a lower-bay Brooklyn node linked to Battery Park and Atlantic Terminal.
Added Union Square as a central Manhattan node linked to Chelsea, Battery Park, and Williamsburg.
Removed the direct Newark Penn -> World Trade route because it overstated a real-world one-seat corridor.
Removed the Newark Airport -> Battery Park route because it read more like a gameplay shortcut than a believable board compression.
Removed t-exchange-grand-central and increased t-grand-central-flushing to strengthen outer-Queens ticket value while easing central-trunk overlap.
Recolored several formerly gray routes so the route palette is less over-concentrated in neutral edges.
Expanded the regular ticket deck from 23 to 28 by adding more outer-node and non-central split objectives around Newark Airport, Flushing, Red Hook, and Hudson Yards.
Collapsed Secaucus -> Penn District from a double route into a single tunnel trunk.
Removed the LIC -> Downtown Brooklyn ferry edge and added Union Square -> Williamsburg as the direct L-train-inspired Manhattan/Brooklyn connector.
playtestFocus: Check whether Battery Park improves the south-core decision space instead of just subdividing one route.
Check whether Grove St improves Jersey-side route identity instead of making the west corridor feel over-segmented.
Check whether Hoboken -> Grove St clarifies west-side choice or over-connects the Jersey cluster.
Check whether Hudson Yards creates a credible west-Manhattan layer without collapsing back into the same Penn District core.
Check whether Flushing adds useful outer-Queens strategy instead of just duplicating the direct Grand Central -> Jamaica corridor.
Check whether Newark Airport still justifies its place once the helicopter-style Hudson Yards tunnel is available but intentionally hard to complete.
Check whether Red Hook improves the south-harbor branch instead of only adding another Brooklyn leaf.
Check whether Union Square adds central decision-making without creating a dense Manhattan knot, and whether its new higher placement reads more clearly than the old south-heavy version.
Preserve good board-space usage while adding west-Manhattan and east-side options.
Avoid adding nodes whose routes only increase congestion without improving strategy.
Review whether the newer, less-gray route palette improves readability without making route color logic feel arbitrary.
